sentences.text: It was the-- the big change was September 1, 1939.
There was a-- the abrupt change, a sudden change.
This was not a gradual, something you get prepared.
As far as-- you mean changes prior to the outbreak of the war, when the war broke out, the changes?
Our first awakening, the actual, both your physical and mental awakening was September 1, 1939, on a Friday morning.
You could wake me tomorrow morning at three in the morning or two and ask me, Henry, when did a war break out.
And I can tell you the time and the day.
Because it's so cemented in my mind.
And that was the rude and shocking awakening of explosion, bomb, or bombs.
Because the windows began to rattle, almost like a earthquake or more so.
And we jumped out of bed.
It was before dawn or right at dawn, Friday morning, September first.
My parents started to-- turned on the light quick.
Looking out the window, we could see smoke.
And my father said, oh, my god, a war broke out.
Then another bomb and another explosion.
And I could see out of my bed, ashen faces, white faces.
And, as a kid, yeah, I wasn't so affected right away.
In fact, I remember-- which seems silly now-- I remember thinking to myself, wow, this is-- this could be exciting.
Until now, I used to see it in the movies.
Wayne-- John Wayne, with cavalry, and Rover 1 pictures with explosions on the western front and all that.
And here we're seeing it live.
And the next thought came to my mind, hey, Monday-- school year is supposed to start Monday.
I was supposed to go in the ninth grade.
No school Monday.
Until a few minutes later, or maybe half hour, hour later, I could see my folks packing.
They said, we better get out of here.
And it was about 8, 9 o'clock when we first went to the bakery to pick up some goods.
They were already closing shop.
No one would-- people were panicking.

And I remember the neighbors who were so friendly, all of a sudden, I remember one neighbor putting on an arm band with a swastika on it and accosting my father with a heil Hitler sign and speaking German all of a sudden.
He forgot Polish overnight.
And I can't quite recall what it was.
But it wasn't complementary.
It was something like, our time has come now, and all that.
So this was a rude awakening.
We started to pack.
And my folks said, we're going-- we have to get out of here, until things calm down.
They had no idea what's going to go-- was it going to be a bombing, or will the Hitlerjugend or the Nazi party, which was until then outlawed, it was underground.
All of a sudden, they came out of the woodwork.
And so we better get out of here.
We go back to Czeladz, which was a neighboring town.
As I said, it was like three to four kilometers.
And we went on foot.
In fact, this is before automobile.
We didn't have a car, unfortunately.
If we had, maybe we could have flee to some other country, if there was such a thing, if they let you in.
But it is dreaming.
So we went to this-- took us perhaps something like a hour and a half, two hours-- we were on foot-- to reach that neighboring town, to Czeladz.
That's where my fraternal grandparents used to live.
And I remember, on the way, we went through open field.
And we were strafed by a German, because there was no Polish-- I remember two, three guys laying with a machine gun in a cornfield-- rye field, actually rye-- and shooting up in the sky.
And we had to be passing by that narrow road with our little-- pulling a little wagon, like kids have a little wagon, except this was bigger.
You pull it by hand.
You put your belongings on it.
And we were sort of marching like.
And all of a sudden, they start shooting at this aircraft.
And in no time, two or three aircrafts came and started circling around us and showering us with machine gun bullets.
So my dad jumped in the ditch.
He said let's go, jump in the ditch real quick.
So we went there in the ditch and lay flat.
And bullets were falling all around us, because of the two or three guys  who were shooting against that airplane.
So we almost got hurt right there.
And so, finally, we go to this town of Czeladz.
And my father looked around and rented a room.
We left everything behind.
We just took what we could carry.
And this was all within a few hours from the time I heard the first bomb exploded.
So when you ask me when I first noticed a change, it's quite a change.
It's like a, god forbid, a tornado or hurri-- something hits.
Except you have very little warning.
So my life started to change.
Goodbye to the apartment, goodbye to my home.
We rented one room and no more school.
It's true.
We have no more school Monday.
And no more school the following Mondays either and months and years to come.
So little did I know what, how I was wishing for.
And, you, then say in German and tell him, this is a new-- things will be different from now on.
So all of a sudden, I felt like a different person.
Well, my friends were-- and what about school, what about home?
And this was a big, radical change.
And you slowly begin, start to cope, and start to regroup, and see what do we do now.
Where do we go from here?
When you went to Czeladz, did you take anything that had special significance for you?
You would like to think when you pack something in a hurry, that you take important things.
I took my ball, my soccer ball, and a couple books.
I had a harmonica, which my uncle bought me before the war.
And I had a little book with music, So I took that-- a shirt, an extra pair of shoes, the essentials, toothbrush.
And so well, we'll be back here anyhow.
It's only temporary, so we thought.
This will blow over.
And maybe the [INAUDIBLE] too, besides the Polish Army is going to beat them back pretty soon.
And we will be back home.
So, little did we know, what was to follow that.
So we took the essentials, very, very little with us.
And we came to Czeladz, and the same story there.
Everybody else is packing.
They're leaving, too.
Where are you going?
Well, the Germans are advancing.
We're close to the German border.
We've got to go deeper into Poland.
How do you go without-- trains are not running, because Polish army confiscated most major train routes.
And you have no automobile.
Buses are not running.
So you walk.
How far can you walk in a day's time, until sundown?
So we walked like that for three days, until the German Army caught up with us.
And that was useless, too.
Actually they caught up the first day, but it just happened they didn't march into that town.
But they were all around us.
They encircled us, anyhow.
So that's when the first few days of living under the German occupation began, which, as you know, lasted for almost a good five and a half years.
We had one room, close to a main street.
My father said, we got to adjust, somehow, to this.
This will not last long, maybe next-- a few weeks, a few months.
And we didn't have any furniture.
So he says, I have to go back to Siemianowice and get the furniture.
This is my furniture.
He came to the apartment-- and I didn't go with him, he went by himself, it was sealed already.
So he went to see the authorities, said, I want my furniture.
I'm moving.
And first they gave him some run around.
But somebody he knew there from the coal miners, too, who was then in the party, and they opened for him.
And he took a horse and buggy, rented from somebody, and took some furniture-- at least a bedroom, and tables and chairs, the essentials-- and moved into Czeladz.
So we had some kind of a semblance of normalcy to start a new household.
So the next thing he said, I got to go back to work.
But how do we-- the coal mines are taken over by the Germans now.
And they established between Czeladz and Siemianowice, which was-- Czeladz was in Poland proper.
They restored the old border, which was before World War I, when Poland took it over in, I believe, in 1918 or '19.
And they put the barriers down.
And my father couldn't go back to Siemianowice-- or Laurahutte.
They changed the name back to Laurahutte.
Like it was before the Germans left.
And so he went to the police station, and asked for a pass.
Because he has to go back to the mines.
He works on the other side in the mines.
And they gave him -- the Polish authorities gave him a mine.
The Polish authorities was then already supervised by the German.
But these were Polish civilian authorities.
And he went to the mine and went to see the manager or whoever was in charge.
And his luck would want it, the same guy, the same management came over that had left a few years earlier to manage the mines.
He knew and they knew him, because he was the only Jew there.
And he asked to go back to his old job.
And they said, they see no problem with that.
And so they give him his old job back, which was he was underground, loading coal, mining coal.
And that meant, of course, actually a plus in our living standard.
Because, first of all, it meant income.
Because, all of a sudden, income was cut off.
You had no money coming in.
And it meant that he could go across the border to Germany proper.
And he could buy things and bring back to Czeladz.
Because about six months later, they started putting Jews all together in one neighborhood and restricting Jews to go to different streets.
It was the first steps of the ghetto.
And my father was fortunate enough to be able to leave the ghetto, go to the German territory-- to the actual proper German territory across the border.
Go to his mine, where he worked so many years, and still got paid in German Reich's mark.
And was able to buy goods at the company store, which meant a lot.
He brought food into the ghetto.
So we were really the envy of a lot of people.
And my father, I remember kept saying, you see, I told you guys, the Germans, they're civilized people.
Look, no one bothers me, you know.
We will survive, lord willing, we shall overcome, you know.
This, too, shall pass.
And after all, the war won't be forever.
We had no idea about how the war was going.
But weeks turned into months, and months before you know it turned into a year.
And I still didn't go to school.
Instead of school, they assigned me forced labor.
But my father kept working on for about almost a year, until the SS authorities and the Nazis took over the mines.
And, of course, they ethnic-cleansed it.
And my father was the first casualty, of course, and they let him go.
That was the end of his career.
And then we ghetto-ized, if there is such a description.
At first, by that time, I was already 15.
Let's see, again, war broke out, I was-- I turned 14 the month after the war broke out.
So a year later, I was 15.
So they gave me a shovel, a broom, a pick to-- a hammer, whatever.
Everything from breaking stones, crushing stones for road, to digging ditches for a sewer line, water line, to in the winter time clearing snow for the Germans' supplies to pass through and all that.
So just manual labor.
One thing come to mind.
Before the ghetto was established, when we came to Czeladz.
I'd like to back up a little bit, because this will have a bearing in my later-- actually affected my later life.
So it was a hot September.
And after my father went back to Siemianowice, got the furniture, we got halfway kind of situated, a few weeks later, high holidays came, 1939.
I don't remember the date.
But must have been late September.
And my father said-- shul was the first casualty.
They burned the shul down, the synagogue down, the first two or three days, when the Germans invaded.
But there were services, he was told, by word of mouth, at [?
Heine ?] Yousef's house and Tall Moshe.
People in the small towns were referred by the scriptures, [?
Heine ?] Yousef or the Tall Moshe, or the Limping Yankel, or whatever.
And Moshe and [?
Heine ?] Yousef owned a duplex.
And the rooms were rather large.
And they rescued the Torah from the burning shul.
And they were having services at the Tall Moshe's house and [?
Heine ?] Yousef's And my father says, let's go to services.
And I says, I don't want to go.
He says,  no, no, you're coming with me.
And so my brother and I-- he took my brother and I to services.
But the services were not like in the shul.
Because this was one large room.
And I would guess, something like maybe 20 by 22, or maybe 18 by 22.
It must have been, in that room, if you let 10, 15 people there, or 20, you could probably sit comfortable.
But there must have been a hundred  of them, maybe over a hundred people.
And there was no-- sitting was out.
If you find a place to stand, you were lucky.
It was like squashed together with a little, bitty place for-- not only [INAUDIBLE]-- but a little, little table, and the Torah was resting on it.
And I remember seeing it.
It was.
So hot and I start perspiring.
And most people perspire.
I can still smell the perspiration today.
And I said, I got to get out of here.
So I went outside.
And my father say, you stay here.
I said, no, no, no.
I got to get some air.
So I went outside.
I wasn't-- my mind wasn't on the services, anyhow, even if it was comfortable inside.
Because, at that point, I thought it was kind of useless.
Look what they're doing to us.
And here these people are standing here and praying, and lamenting.
And I could see the [INAUDIBLE].
I could still see the movement, the rocking movement of the moving the heads and the [INAUDIBLE].
I went to the courtyard and looked up.
And there was a staircase, too, from the outside to go up.
And I see three or four girls looking up.
And they are giggling from the window.
And they're kind of cute looking girls.
So, I said, hey, why don't you come down, let's talk a little bit.
And they started giggling and laughing.
And then another guy joined me, and he says, yeah, good idea.
What's your name?
And they were just giggling.
So what I remember distinctly is she caught  my eye-- beautiful girl, beautiful dark eyes, dark hair, pigtails, gosh, complexion like peach and cream.
And I said, oh gosh, what a beau-- this is god's greatest creation here.
And I says, what's you name, come on down.
So she says, [?
Yahzhah. ?] And I told her my name.
So I said, I got to remember that name.
So, finally, after about a half a hour, another guy joins.
So they came down.
We talked, and so I introduced myself, and I told them my name.
And we told, we said, we should get together sometimes.
And we talked a little more about what school and what grade are you in.
Turned out they were younger than I was, by two years, and some of them three years.
So that was-- that's what I remember about services.
But I do remember the girl.
And, of course, later on I start looking for her.
And I found her in the ghetto, and we struck up a friendship.
I, perhaps, will talk a little later on about that.
But, in meantime, I would like to answer your question about the ghetto life and what I remember about the first services.
This was-- it really stands out in my mind.
The first High Holiday service in what was then already beginning to be a ghetto.
And I'll still-- coming back to my work, this is what I did the first, perhaps, year.
Then they drafted me into a factory, into a ceramic factor-- actually my dad, too, and my brother.
And they took about 30 or 40 of us.
And we worked-- there I spend about two years, two, perhaps, two and 1/2 years, in the ceramic factory.
It was actually hard physical work, but it did have a plus.
The plus was that this work was important work for the German building industry, such as they needed schools and hospitals.
And we were making toilets and wash basins out of ceramic, out of clay.
There were kilns, fired with coal, and then load into the kilns.
And then as it would burn the certain degrees, empty it out, and real hot.
And we would have to have gloves, to even touch the hot contents.
And it was hard physical work.
But we would get one meal a day in the factory.
And the director of the factory was a German.
He took over the factory from-- because before there were the factory was a Jewish factory.
And, as I recall, now as I reflect and I think back of this German, he was a mean looking guy.
He would wear-- he was a chemist by trade.
He would wear a white-- you would think he's a doctor-- a white coat, and run through the halls of the factory, because there were big long halls.
And everybody would really move, because he had a mean look.
And he was always accompanied by either a uniformed guy, you know.
And everybody would just freeze when his steel eyes looking at you.
But, later on, as it turned out to be, the steel eyes and all this Prussian, authoritative look, was perhaps only a front.
Because deep inside, he was a good human being.
Later, into now, like 1942, when deportations began out of the ghetto.
They were taking people left and right.
He would give a special papers, stating that we are doing very important work, and we are an important labor force, and we are very much needed.
So they left us alone.
So, he actually was trying, and to a certain degree, saved our lives, as long as he could.
And the work itself, even though it was physical, it was-- I was young.
I was then 15 and 16.
And, of course, Saturdays we had free, and Sunday so we would-- even in the ghetto, we would eke out some kind of a social life.
You know, kids always find a way of surviving somehow.
And I always wanted to meet with the girls, with the boys and strike up a friendship with [?
Yahzhah. ?] And we would meet in someone's apartment-- apartment, I mean, this one room.
They would go out for the afternoon and let us kids in.
And one of them had the old record player, gramophone.
We would wind it up and put this record on there.
I used to love to listen to on the console radio.
It was the same type music I used to listen to from BBC.
It was like Benny Goodman and Glenn Miller, Tex Beneke.
And it was a different type of music.
And we would try to make up some steps and try to learn how to dance to what we thought was a strange, crazy kind of stuff.
And this is how we'd spend our social part of the ghetto life.
And then I was asked to play a harmonica or sing a song.
And we would dance a little bit.
And those were are the pleasant memories of kids and the ghetto.
And, of course, [?
Yahzhah ?] and I became friends, close friends then.
Not real close, not sanctioned by their parents, god forbid.
Because they would be mad if-- what do mean, you're 16.
You're already going with a girl, or steady?
And we couldn't go to a theater or movie.
You couldn't leave the ghetto.
But this was more or less our rendezvous, our place to meet.
So I would look forward to-- and this hard work in the factory-- but came weekend, two things to look forward to.
At noon in the factory for the meal, they would give us a soup with some potatoes in it and a slice of bread.
And then our weekend meeting with other kids.
Those are the two things kept me going.
How would you describe the physical look of the ghetto?
The physical look was at first glance-- and probably second or third glance, too-- sorrowful.
Because it was a dilapidated section of town.
The Germans would choose a section of town which was run down, houses with leaky roofs.
I don't want to categorize our city here.
But we have sections in this city where-- South Central or places with houses which are in badly need of repair.
That's where they would put the Jews in a section like that, houses without even water or sewers.
And Jewish people who had homes, comfortable homes-- we had a comfortable apartment-- would naturally vacate that and move into one room.
And sometimes they would put two-- if there were not enough rooms to go around, they would put two families in it.
If there was a single family or a childless family or a family with one child, they would put two of them in the room, which would be a very cramped space.
But that's how you lived in the ghetto.
And later came the fence.
You couldn't go across the fence.
Or even without a fence-- this is the street.
This is the boundary.
And at curfew time, they would line out-- put various-- I'm thinking about billboards, like not our big street billboards, but billboards on houses, and say, this is a new law.
So-and-so street is not to be frequented by Jews.
And the time, starting up in April or May so-and-so, you're supposed to be indoors at 7 PM.
And, of course, in the winter time, it was 6 PM, or 5, whatever.
So curfew and restricted movement were the most despicable acts of dehumanizing.
